coredata.dc:title: Improvement power conversion efficiency in graphene quantum dot by functionalization and heteroatom doping used in solar cell
coredata.dc:creator: Saadh, Mohamed J.
Flores, Juan José
Dayeh, Ghassan Ali
Yadav, Anupam
Kattab, Nada Othman
Salman, Noor Abd Alkhudhur
Zainul, Rahadian
Abbas, Mohamed
Pandey, Shatrudhan
coredata.dc:description: First-principles density functional theory (DFT) was adopted for investigating the influence of adatoms (S, Si, Al) upon the graphene quantum dot functionalized with the carboxyl group (CO2H-GQD) in order to find novel materials due to unique optical (redshift) and electronic (lower bandgap) properties for utilization in quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs). For the sake of examining the alteration in the electronic attributes due to the insertion of adatoms, the bandgaps, the LUMO and the HOMO were examined using the density functional B3LYP and the basis set 6-31G. For the sake of examining the charge separation and electron injection in the un-doped and doped CO2H-GQD, we scrutinized the charge transport, molecular electrostatic potential (MESP) and the binding mechanism. The optical attributes demonstrated a broad spectrum in the visible range favorable towards harvesting solar light. We also investigated the parameters of solar cells such as efficiency (η), short circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF), open circuit voltage (Voc) in order for the sake of examining the use of adatom-doped CO2H-GQD in QDSCs. There was an increase in the efficiency of S-doped, Si-doped, and Al-doped CO2H-GQD. Doping the CO2H-GQD led to the maximum efficiency since electron donating nature was more, thereby injecting more electrons in the surface of TiO2. The results demonstrated that such GQD-based sensitizers can be used effectively in QDSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Solar cells
Open circuit voltage
Maximum efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of the pristine and doped structural of GQDs
Solar cell parameters and optical attributes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized geometries of (a) CO2H edge-functionalized GQD, (b) Al@CO2H-GQD (c) Si@CO2H-GQD (d) S@CO2H-GQD.
HOMO-LUMO plots for doped/un-doped CO2H edge-functionalized GQDs.
MESP plots for doped/un-doped CO2H edge-functionalized GQDs.
Calculated solar cell parameters of considered systems along with HOMO, LUMO energies, HOMO-LUMO gap (Eg), electron affinity (EA) and work function (WF).
Calculated maximum absorption wavelength, λmax (nm), electronic transition energy (eV), oscillator strength (f), and light harvesting energy (LHE) of all considered systems.
Calculated solar cell parameters of all considered systems.

head.ce:title: Improvement power conversion efficiency in graphene quantum dot by functionalization and heteroatom doping used in solar cell

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: First-principles density functional theory (DFT) was adopted for investigating the influence of adatoms (S, Si, Al) upon the graphene quantum dot functionalized with the carboxyl group (CO2H-GQD) in order to find novel materials due to unique optical (redshift) and electronic (lower bandgap) properties for utilization in quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs). For the sake of examining the alteration in the electronic attributes due to the insertion of adatoms, the bandgaps, the LUMO and the HOMO were examined using the density functional B3LYP and the basis set 6-31G. For the sake of examining the charge separation and electron injection in the un-doped and doped CO2H-GQD, we scrutinized the charge transport, molecular electrostatic potential (MESP) and the binding mechanism. The optical attributes demonstrated a broad spectrum in the visible range favorable towards harvesting solar light. We also investigated the parameters of solar cells such as efficiency (η), short circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF), open circuit voltage (Voc) in order for the sake of examining the use of adatom-doped CO2H-GQD in QDSCs. There was an increase in the efficiency of S-doped, Si-doped, and Al-doped CO2H-GQD. Doping the CO2H-GQD led to the maximum efficiency since electron donating nature was more, thereby injecting more electrons in the surface of TiO2. The results demonstrated that such GQD-based sensitizers can be used effectively in QDSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the viable solutions for meeting the dire need for clean energies is to use third generation photovoltaics (PVs) owing to their electricity generation and cost-effectiveness [1–5]. In addition, researchers have turned their attention to cost-effective solar cells, for example quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs), thanks to their superior attributes like higher absorption coefficient and shape-size tunable gaps [6–12]. Solar cells based on CdTe, CdSe, cesium lead iodide (CsPbI3) and colloidal lead sulfide (PbS) quantum dots have widespread applications as QDSCs because they can suppress Shockley-Queisser limit and they have size-dependent optical attributes [13–17]. Although the efficiency of such QDSCs is high, they contain metals such as cadmium and lead, which are highly toxic. Therefore, finding alternatives that are environment-friendly, biocompatible and less toxic is of utmost importance to fabricate solar cells. Three device compositions have been considered to investigate the important attributes of QDSCs, namely quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs), organic and Schottky junction solar cells. [7,18–23].

In fact, these solar cells are identical to dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs). However, in DSCs, PV occurs between dye metal oxide semiconductors and redox electrolytes. Thus, they have caught the attention of researchers for application in solar cells. Moreover, researchers have extensively investigated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) since they have widespread applications such as in light emission, bio-imaging and optoelectronics [24]. GQDs are biocompatible, highly stable and nontoxic, which has caused researchers to use them in DSCs [24]. Additionally, GQDs have been considered for application in solar cells thanks to their high light harvesting ability, size-dependent bandgap tailoring, edge impacts as well as quantum confinement. For example, there are various research studies into different types of solar cells that use carbon dots (CDs) and GQDs [25]. Thus far, GQDs have been utilized as heterojunction solar cells, semiconductor/GQDs and HTL materials. In a piece of research by Zhang et al. [26], 0.13 % power conversion efficiency (PCE) was obtained for nitrogen (N)-doped CDs. In a recent work by Wang et al. [27], an N-doped solar cell based on CDs was designed with the PCE of 0.79 %. Furthermore, the PCE of QDSCs can be further enhanced. A recent study has been also carried out on the impact of four heteroatoms upon the optoelectronic attributes GQDs [28]. Additionally, one of the effective methods for engineering the optical and electron transfer attributes is considered to be heteroatom doping, which causes electronic and structural distortions. Doping a foreign atom is capable of impacting the overall electrical distributions of GQDs, which is capable of tuning the optoelectronic attributes.

Within the current study, first-principles based on density functional theory (DFT) was utilized to investigate the electronic attributes and the solar cell parameters of carboxyl-functionalized GQDs doped with S, Si, and Al atoms. In the method employed in this study, a carbon atom of carboxyl-functionalized GQDs was replaced with S, Si and Al for the sake of improving the electronic, structural and optoelectronic [29,30] attributes as well as the performance of GQDs for application in QDSCs.

The impact of doping the adatoms S, Si and Al on the CO2H-GQDs was investigated through DFT computations. The density functional B3LYP [31–33] with the basis set 6-31G was used to carry out the geometry optimizations of doped and un-doped GQDs using GAMESS software [34]. In order to verify minima structures with no imaginary frequencies, vibrational frequencies were computed. Additionally, the structures were optimized again for single point calculations at both un-doped and doped GQDs in order to achieve precision in the protocol. Gauss View program was utilized to visualize HOMOs, the LUMOs and the bandgap (Eg). The self-consistent field convergence criterion was established at 10−6. Additionally, for optimizations, the maximum displacement, indicating the maximum structural change of a single coordinate, and the average (RMS) change across all structural parameters, were set at approximately 1.8 × 10−4 and 1.2 × 10−4, respectively. The maximum remaining force on an atom in the system, as well as the average root mean square (RMS) force on all atoms, was set to approximately 4.5 × 10−4 and 3.0 × 10−4, respectively. The MESP is visualized as a color-coded map, where different colors represent different levels of potential. Regions of high negative potential (electron-rich areas) were depicted in red, while regions of high positive potential (electron-poor areas) were depicted in blue. MESP analysis helps in understanding the reactivity and behavior of molecules. For example, nucleophilic or electron-rich regions tend to have high negative potentials and are attracted to positively charged species, while electrophilic or electron-poor regions tend to have high positive potentials and are attracted to negatively charged species.

Time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) computations were further undertaken within the framework of IEFPCM following the optimization to investigate UV–visible absorption spectra and the electronic transitions. The self-consistent reaction field (SCRF) method along with the Polarizable Continuum Model (PCM) was utilized to take into account the solvent influence in the calculations. PCM executes reaction field calculations through IEFPCM [35], thus generating the solute cavities via overlapping spheres. The impact of the solvent is investigated regarding its macroscopic attributes such as surface tension and dielectric constant (ε) at 298 K. The solvent used in this study was water with a dielectric constant of 78.3. Moreover, we expressed the water polarization with regard to the surface charge density on the surface of the cavity that produced an electrical field in the cavity that has an impact upon the solute [36]. UV–vis was computed with the inclusion of the solvent (water). Solar cell parameters such as efficiency (η), short circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF), and open circuit voltage (Voc) were calculated for checking the possibility of using un-doped and doped GQDs in QDSCs and efficiency (η) was computed as follows [37]:
(1)
η =
FF
J sc
V oc
P in

Other parameters such as Voc, FF Jsc were computed as follows [38]:
(2)
V oc
=
E LUMO GQDs
−
E CB
TiO 2

Voc can be defined as the maximum voltage from solar cells occurring at a zero current. Voc signifies the differences between the LUMOs of the conduction bands (CB) of acceptor TiO2 and the donor (GQDs). Voc is dependent upon the light-generated current and the saturation current. In addition, Voc can be used to measure the recombination amount in devices.
(3)
FF =
ϑ oc
− ln
ϑ oc
+ 0.72
ϑ oc
+ 1
;
ϑ oc
=
q ×
V oc
k B
T

FF assesses the maximum power obtained from the solar cells and it is related to Voc and Isc. FF could be estimated through Eq. (3), where υoc signifies the normalized Voc [39]. LHE is one of the important parameters in improving Jsc for the sake of identifying the efficiency of QDSCs. The higher the LHE is, the higher the efficiency will be. The fraction of light intensities absorbed at specific wavelengths in QDSCs or DSSCs is defined as absorptance (A) or LHE [40].
(4)
LHE = A =
I abs
I o

Here Iabs signifies the absorbed intensity and Io signifies the incident intensity. Jsc signifies the current density at zero voltage, which was computed as follows:
(5)
J sc
=
∫ o ∞
q
A
E
I sun
E
dE
where Isun (E) signifies the photon flux density and q signifies the electron charge. Jsc is dependents upon several factors such as optical attributes, incident light spectrum and solar cell area. In The ECB of TiO2 was considered to be −4.0 eV in the current calculations [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the pristine and doped structural of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structure and attributes of GQD were investigated before and following the doping of Al, Si, and S atoms to gain an understanding into the rationality of the computations. We modeled an electron-donating coronene molecule as a GQD whose edges were ended by 2 carboxyl groups, namely CO2H-GQD. There was a photoinduced electron transfer to the surface of acceptor TiO2 from the donor GQD. The increase of electrons from the ground state of GQD to the CB of TiO2 (excited state) was because of the absorbed photon. Later, excited electron had an injection in the CB of TiO2 via different adiabatic or non- adiabatic processes. The optimized structures of CO2H-GQD and three adatoms can be seen Fig. 1 . The length of the optimized bond between the C atoms in CO2H-GQD was ∼1.43 Å. Generally, the CC bond and CO bond in the benzene rings connected to carboxyl groups are in the range of 1.49 to 1.52 Å, and 1.209 to 1.217 Å respectively [42]. Moreover, the CC bond angle was 122.19°. Nevertheless, owing to the repulsion between the benzene ring and carboxyl group in GQD, there was an increase of 1.95° in the angle.

In Fig. 1 the impact of the substitutional doping in CO2H-GQD was shown. The bond length of the CAl was 1.94 Å in Al@CO2H-GQDs after doping the Al atom (see Fig. 1 (b)) in CO2H -GQD. In a similar vein, the bond length of CSi was 1.91 Å in Si@CO2H-GQDs after doping the Si atom (see Fig. 1 (c)). The computed bond distance between CSi and CAl atoms in this study are similar to those reported in the literature [43]. The similar atomic radius of carbon, Al, and Si atoms can be reason for the less difference in the bond distance between them. In addition, doping the S atom in CO2H-GQD (see Fig. 1(d)) distorted the hexagonal ring, thus increasing the length of the CS bond up to ∼1.78 Å. The insertion of adatoms disrupted the sp2 hybridization of the C atoms, which changed the structure of GQD, thereby changing the electronic attributes of CO2H-GQD significantly. Furthermore, for comparing the stability of these various structures, the binding energy of un-doped and doped CO2H-GQDs was computed as follows:
(6)
for undoped :
E b
=
nE
C
+
nE
H
+
nE
O
−
E
CO 2
H − GQD
N
(7)
for doped :
E b
=
nE
C
+
nE
H
+
nE
O
+
nE
x
−
E
x @
CO 2
H − GQD
N

Here, the energy of one oxygen, hydrogen and carbon is signified by E(O), E(H) and E(C) respectively. N signifies the number of atoms in the systems, E(CO2H-GQD) and E(x@COOH-GQD) signify the total energies of the un-doped and doped CO2H-GQDs. The binding energies of doped and un-doped CO2H-GQD are provided in Table 1 . The binding energy for every atom for CO2H-GQD was 5.23 eV, which increased following the doping. The computed binding energy values in Table 1 were all positive, which confirmed that all structures were stable. Nonetheless, the structural attributes of the systems doped with adatoms could explain their identical binding energies (Eb). Eb was the same for all systems since the ionic radius of the dopants was the same with small changes in the bond length between CAl and CSi. Nevertheless, small distortions in the hexagonal ring changed the bond length from ∼1.43 to 1.76 Å in the case of S doping, thus leading to a higher binding energy than the others (Eb = 7.28 eV). Moreover, the dipole moments for un-doped and doped COOH-GQDs are provided in Table 1. There was an increase in the dipole moment due to the doping in CO2H-GQD.The dipole moment of CO2H-GQD doped with the S atom was the highest (3.37 Debye), which could be ascribed to the structural change that generated differences between the local dipoles. The distortion in the hexagonal structure near the S atom in CO2H-GQD could be the reason for the rise in the dipole moment of CO2H-GQD doped with S atom. The symmetry in the π-electrons of GQD was broken after doping the S atom in CO2H-GQD, thus leading to a higher dipole moment. Additionally, the difference in electronegativity value of C and S contributed to dipole moment formation and charge re-distribution [44].

The LUMO and HOMO energies and the bandgap (Eg) for all the systems are listed in Table 1. The changes in HOMO-LUMO energies in Table 1 and Fig. 2 show the impact of adatoms upon the electronic attributes. The bandgap was reduced after doping the adatoms. The bandgap was in the following order: S@CO2H-GQD (3.28 eV) < Si@CO2H-GQD (3.49 eV) < Al@CO2H-GQD (3.68 eV) < CO2H-GQD (3.99 eV). As shown in Fig. 2, the LUMO energies of all systems were more than the CB of TiO2 (−4.0 eV) [45]. This demonstrated the possibility efficient electron injection from the excited states of the un-doped and doped CO2H-GQD to the CB of TiO2. Moreover, the I−/I3− redox couple of −4.8 eV was higher than the HOMO energies [46]. This possibly demonstrated the effective and rapid regeneration process.

The molecular electrostatic potential (MESP), which is the potential experienced by a positive charge unit near the molecules because of their electron density distribution, was computed to gain an understanding into the relationship between the activity and structure of the molecules. MESP demonstrated the chemical reactivities as negative and positive potentials, which anticipate the sites for nucleophilic and electrophilic attacks respectively. The MESP of the systems in the planes of the base ring can be seen in Fig. 3 . The MESP of all CO2H-GQDs were mapped on the electron density surface of 0.0168 unit. The blue and red colors in Fig. 3 demonstrate the positive and negative electrostatic potentials respectively. The potential in MESP was as follows: blue>green>yellow>orange>red. The zero potential region is the green surface. CO2H-GQD exhibited more negative electrostatic potential after doping the adatoms, which made them ideal for the injection of electrons.

Moreover, the workfunction of doped/un-doped CO2H-GQD was computed to understand the impact of inserting the adatoms upon the electronic attributes of CO2H-GQDs. The least amount of energy needed for removing an electron present from a solid surface is defined as the workfunction, which was computed as follows [47]:
(8)
WF =
IE + EA
2
where IE = -HOMO signifies the ionization energy and EA = -LUMO signifies the electron affinity. There was a dramatic change in the WF for CO2H-GQD following the doping. The WF of un-doped CO2H-GQD was 3.96 eV. It remained almost the same after doping the Al atom, but it increased negligibly after the Si atom was doped. The insertion of S atom dramatically increased the WF of CO2H-GQD. The workfunction values are provided in Table 1. The changes in the workfunction can further explain the charge transport between the dopants and COOH-GQD

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar cell parameters and optical attributes
ce:section.ce:para: The maximum absorption wavelengths based on UV–visible spectra for the systems at 10 excited states are provided in Table 2 . The absorption spectra of all donors (CO2H-GQD, x@CO2H-GQD) must be similar to the solar spectrum for harvesting more photons. All of the absorption spectra existed in the visible region for all the systems. The singlet-singlet electron transition along with the oscillator strength (OS) was computed to investigate the energy and nature of electronic transitions [48]. The OS provided in Table 2 revealed the possibility of emission and absorption in the transitions between the HOMO level and the LUMO level [49].

The main electronic transition contribution in our closed-shell CO2H-GQD for the highest peak wavelength and OS was because of the H1 → LUMO (17 %), HOMO→L + 1(83 %) and H-2 → L + 2 (4 %), H-1 → L + 1 (6 %) respectively [24]. As shown in Table 2, doping the adatoms changed the absorption attributes of CO2H-GQD. The results obtained are similar to those obtained in the literature [50].

The negligible change in results compared to the experimental results can be because of other factors such as the shape, size and the concentration of the carboxyl group connected the edges of GQD. Since the optoelectronic attributes of GQDs are highly dependent upon their shape and size, the UV–visible peak will change based on the optical attributes [51]. This transition might be because of n → π* transition because of CO. There was a red-shift in the peaks because of doping Al atom, Si atom and S atom in the following order: CO2H-GQD (308 nm) < Al@CO2H- GQD (332 nm) < Si@CO2H-GQD (355 nm) < S@CO2H-GQD (398 nm), which was similar to the previous theoretical result [28]. This could be because the radius and valence electrons in Si and S atoms were large and extra, which changed the electronic attributes of CO2H-GQDs. Amongst the systems, the absorption of S-doped CO2H-GQD at 398 nm was the strongest, which demonstrated more light-harvesting ability at longer wavelengths [52], which further increased the PCE of the S-doped CO2H-GQD.

The solar cell parameters of both doped and un-doped CO2H-GQD mentioned above, namely η, FF, Jsc and Voc, were computed in order to evaluate the possibility of using QDSCs solar cells. The TiO2 photoanode has attracted considerable attention in order to fabricate highly efficient and cost-effective photovoltaic devices for application in solar cells. TiO2 is considered significant amongst different metal oxides utilized in QDSCs due to its high charge collecting behavior [52]. In addition to its cost-effectiveness, TiO2 is environment-friendly and abundant in nature. Synthesizing GQDs on TiO2 can lead to an active charge injection to TiO2 from GQDs [53]. Based on the literature, various functional groups can be used to intensify the electronic coupling between GQDs and TiO2 [53]. The current study, the donor (CO2H-GQD) was connected to the surface of TiO2 via CO2H at the edges, which led to a high electron injection. Furthermore, by analyzing the electronic attributes of both un-doped and doped systems, the LUMO energies were found to be higher than the CB of TiO2, which showed that the injection of electrons from the excited states of the systems to the CB of TiO2 was faster. The HOMO energies were below the I−/I3− redox couple, which indicated that the regeneration process was efficient and rapid. Several research studies have been done on nanoscale carbon materials such as GQDs, CNTs and Gr with TiO2 because of their high light-harvesting ability and fast charge separation [54].

A dramatic charge separation on TiO2 and high electron injection can be achieved to different electronic levels of un-doped and doped CO2H-GQDs. In order to analyze the possibility of using the results in QDSCs, the TiO2 nanocrystalline surface was considered since the LUMO energy of both un-doped and doped CO2H-GQDs was above the CB of TiO2 and the HOMO energy of both were below the redox couple of I−/I3−. The faster injection of electron by the CO2H-GQDs on TiO2 can be conducive to increasing the PCE of QDSCs. The LHE, oscillator strength (f) and the electronic transition energies of the systems are provided in Table 2. We can compute the LHE as follows [55]:
(9)
LHE = 1 −
10
− f
Here, the oscillator strength was achieved from TD-DFT computations. The LHE values for the systems were as follows: COOH-GQD (0.84) > S@CO2H-GQD (0.46) > Si@CO2H-GQD (0.44) > Al@CO2H-GQD (0.28). The LHE of all doped systems was slightly different, thus leading to similar photocurrents in solar cells.

The parameters for the solar cell for CO2H-GQDs are provided in Table 3 . One of the important parameters specified by the difference between the CB of TiO2 and the LUMO of the donor is Voc. The Voc of CO2H-GQDs is dependent upon the LUMO levels of doped systems. The Voc value for the systems was in the range of 1.23 to 2.03 eV. The positive value of Voc demonstrated that the transfer of electrons from the doped CO2H-GQD to TiO2 was easy. W Jsc was the highest in S@CO2H-GQD and it was the lowest in theun-doped CO2H-GQD. The Jsc, FF and efficiency values of un-doped CO2H-GQDs were 0.59 mA/cm2, 0.914 and 0.5 % respectively. After doping the Al atom, the efficiency increased to 0.82 %. Additionally, the efficiency increased to 0.93 % after doping the S atom, which might be due to the fact that the injection of electrons on the surface of TiO2 was more. The results suggested that the S-doped CO2H-GQD was the most ideal candidate for utilization in solar cells.

The impact of doping three adatoms, namely S, Si and Al, was investigated upon the PCE of CO2H edge-functionalized GQDs was investigated with DFT calculations. Based on the results, there was an increase in the PCE of CO2H edge-functionalized GQDs after doping the abovementioned adatoms since the changed the optoelectronic properties. Moreover, there was a change in the HOMO and LUMO energy levels as well as the bandgap of the doped systems, which caused a change in the photoelectric attributes. The bandgap was computed to be 3.99 eV, 3.68 eV, 3.49 eV and 3.28 eV, respectively, for CO2H-GQD, Al@CO2H-GQD, Si@CO2HGQD and S@CO2H-GQD. The binding mechanism could provide useful insights into designing solar cells with high performance. The highest absorption at 398 nm for S@CO2H-GQD suggested greater light-harvesting ability at longer wavelengths. The decrease in the optical bandgap showed that GQDs can be utilized in optoelectronics. Voc values related to the TiO2 of un-doped and doped CO2H-GQDs were high enough to inject electrons from the donor to the acceptor. S@CO2H-GQD had the greatest Jsc, which could be thanks to the smaller bandgap and higher HOMO energy levels in comparison with Al@CO2H-GQD and Si@CO2HGQD. The increase in the PCE was in the following order: S@CO2H-GQD > Si@CO2H-GQD > Al@CO2H-GQD > CO2H-GQD. Because the electron injection was high in TiO2, the PCE of S@CO2H-GQD was the highest. The results demonstrated that insertion of adatoms in CO2H-GQD made it possible to be used in QDSCs.

Juan José Flores: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Ghassan Ali Dayeh: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Anupam Yadav: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Nada Othman Kattab: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology. Noor Abd Alkhudhur Salman: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Rahadian Zainul: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mohamed Abbas: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software. Shatrudhan Pandey: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University (KKU) for funding this research through the Research Group Program Under the Grant Number: (R.G.P.1/431/44).
